text: Hello, I'm sharing a routine laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
This was done on a patient who had had multiple attacks of acute cholecystitis and finally presented for elective surgery.
The surgeon observes typical adhesions of the gastrocolic omentum attached to the body of the gallbladder, noting that the gallbladder appears less inflamed, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the gastrocolic omentum to expose the bottom of the gallbladder, ensuring the duodenum is safely positioned away from the planned dissection area.
With the adhesions removed, the surgeon begins the dissection at the bottom of the gallbladder, ensuring the duodenum is positioned safely away from the dissection area.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the bottom of the gallbladder, revealing the anticipated location of the cystic duct near the ursulcus.

The surgeon begins dissection on the patient's right side of the gallbladder, continuing the procedure after addressing adhesions and ensuring the duodenum is safely positioned away from the dissection area.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to enhance retraction and manipulation of the gallbladder, which is particularly beneficial for gallbladders with a short mesentery or those that are intrahepatic.
The surgeon moves to the left or medial side of the gallbladder, continuing the dissection upwards in a similar manner to the previous approach on the right side.
The surgeon retracts the gallbladder to facilitate the dissection of the cystic artery and duct, aiming to achieve the critical view of safety.
As a principle, I try not to cauterize anything that I cannot see through.
And I try not to cauterize vessels that I anticipate to be large enough that would warrant a clip.
The surgeon creates a window behind the gallbladder and releases the artery, allowing it to drop back into the hepatocystic triangle to achieve the critical view of safety.
You can see how things are coming into view.
The surgeon meticulously cleans the cystic duct and artery, ensuring clear visibility of the hepatocystic triangle to confirm no other structures are obscured or inadvertently pulled up.
The surgeon meticulously cleans the hepatocystic triangle, ensuring no hidden branches or accessory ducts are present, and avoiding confusion with the common bile duct.
The surgeon exposes the cystic duct and artery, achieving a clear critical view of safety within the hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and then proceeds to clip the cystic artery, ensuring secure closure of these structures within the hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, detaching it from the surrounding tissue after ensuring the cystic duct and artery are safely clipped.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally on the peritoneum, revealing the upper portion of the gallbladder that still requires dissection.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the plane between the gallbladder and the liver bed, which is clearly visible and not obscured by inflammation.
The surgeon reaches the top of the gallbladder and places an instrument behind it, using the instrument as a surface to cauterize the tissue.
After separating the gallbladder, the surgeon positions the camera at the subxiphoid port to facilitate the introduction of a specimen retrieval bag through the paraumbilical port.
In a case like this, you probably could have avoided using the specimen bag altogether because there's no active inflammation. I'll go ahead and we'll take that out.
And that concludes the case. It took about seventeen minutes. The patient did very well. I hope that you enjoyed.
